hero.h1: A mock payment gateway for integration engineers shipping multi-PSP setups
hero.sub: Sandboxes return the response the caller asks for. This one returns the response a merchant would actually see in production — real decline codes, 3DS flows, latency, webhook delivery

section_what.label: What it is
section_what.heading: One tool that emulates five kinds of payment processors
section_what.body: A Python package and REST API that models five processor archetypes — global acquirer, regional bank processor, APM specialist, cross-border FX specialist, high-risk / orchestrator. Each has its own approval rates, decline codes, latency, 3DS behaviour and fees, fitted from a 10,000-row synthetic dataset

section_why.label: The problem it solves
section_why.heading: Test environments don't reflect production, so routing bugs show up when customers complain
points: Stripe's test mode always approves. You cannot ask "what happens when a global acquirer soft-declines a Brazilian Visa at $300?" — the sandbox is not wired to answer it
Every processor's sandbox behaves differently. Regression-testing routing logic across four or five providers means maintaining four or five test harnesses
Edge cases only surface in production. 3DS challenges, country-specific declines, soft-decline retries, webhook races — none reproduce in sandbox

section_who.label: Who it's for
section_who.heading: Integration engineers at merchants, and QA teams shipping routing changes
section_who.body: Primary user: an integration engineer evaluating or maintaining a multi-PSP setup. Also useful for PSP solutions engineers, product managers comparing providers, and data engineers generating training data for retry models

section_what_it_does.label: What the API returns
section_what_it_does.heading: Three things the sandboxes don't
section_what_it_does.heading_lead: Each capability is a concrete transaction path you can hit from the command line or a test suite
points: Realistic decline codes by country and brand. A Brazilian Visa at $300 on the global-acquirer archetype returns ISO 8583 code 05 at the frequency that archetype actually returns it — not a 200 OK
Full payment state machine in Postgres. PENDING to AUTHORIZED to CAPTURED or VOIDED to REFUNDED, strictly validated. Capturing a declined transaction returns 409 with the valid next states
HMAC-signed webhooks that survive restarts. Register URL + secret; events fire with X-Signature-256 and retry on 1-2-4-8-16s backoff. Duplicate requests hit a Redis idempotency cache

section_how_to_use.label: Try it yourself
section_how_to_use.heading: Install it, hit the endpoint
section_how_to_use.body: Three lines gets you a running simulator plus one comparison call:
section_how_to_use.code:
pip install payment-routing-simulator
prs serve &
curl -X POST localhost:8080/api/compare -d '{"country":"BR","brand":"visa","amount":300}'

section_case_study.label: Run the replay
section_case_study.heading: Run the included replay on a committed dataset in 30 seconds
section_case_study.body: One script replays a $50M synthetic DTC apparel book through two routing configs — a single global-acquirer baseline, and a per-corridor archetype selection with soft-decline cascade, network tokens, and EEA SCA exemptions. The CSV is committed, so the output is identical on every run. Full decomposition, pattern IDs, and SQL in case_study.pdf
section_case_study.pull_quote: +2.3 pp authorization, 22% soft-decline salvage within 24 hours, -18 bps effective fee. Every number traces to a CSV column

section_howbuilt.label: Stack
section_howbuilt.heading: FastAPI in front of Postgres, Redis, Kafka and Celery, behind Caddy
section_howbuilt.body_part1: FastAPI validates the API key, checks Redis for idempotency, and passes a sliding-window rate limit. The engine loads archetype YAML, samples approval against country and brand modifiers, and draws latency from a log-normal fit. Every state transition writes to Postgres and publishes to Kafka. Celery workers HMAC-sign the payload and post with exponential backoff
section_howbuilt.body_part2: The chatbot at ivanantonov.com/chatbot calls this simulator's /recommend-route endpoint to rank archetypes by expected approval, latency and fee

byline: Built by Ivan Antonov — ex-ING Payments PM, Yuno payment-ops strategy

right_panel.title: Compare archetypes
right_panel.subtitle: Pick a comparison to see the request and the ranked response the live /api/compare endpoint returns

chips.request: Idempotency key hit on duplicate /simulate within 3s
US Visa $1,200 corporate card with Level-3 line-item data
